free_text: Structure of 12N using 11C+p resonance scattering
DR
BP: Corrected subentries 2-3, added DOI,
separated metadata for the both experiments.
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory's
(LBNL) 88-inch cyclotron.
Berkeley: 10-MeV proton cyclotron was used as a
driver accelerator, producing 11C, which was then
transported as CO2 and injected into the Advanced ECR
ion source (AECR-U).
A DE (thickness 72 microm)-E (thickness 3 mm)
Si-detector telescope was mounted on one scattering
chamber arm, about 14.6 cm away from the target at
Berkeley
Berkeley: The target ladder held a 12.4-mg/cm2-thick
nickel degrader followed by a 18.4-mg/cm2-thick (CH2)n
(= polyethylene) foil.
Thick target inverse kinematics (TTIK) method
at Berkeley includes two measurements. a) The setup
for the 2.2- to 6.6-MeV excitation energy interval:
Measurements at 0 degree were made possible by using
the thick target inverse kinematics (TTIK) method.
In addition to 0 degree data, measurements at 15
degree in the laboratory were also carried out.
b) The setup for the 6.5- to 11.0-MeV excitation energy
interval Measurements were performed at small angles
(0, 5, and 10 degree in the laboratory) to maximize
the counting statistics.
Uncertainties related to the absolute
normalizations are not included in the given error
bars; includes some uncertainty in the corresponding
proton background.
For 5,10
degr. data taken from Figs. 3 to 5.
For 0 and
15 degree, data received from K. Perajarvi on 2/27/2007
BP: Corrected subentry metadata.
K500 superconducting cyclotron
TAM: a 11C secondary beam, with a beam purity of 99.9%.
A parallel plate avalanche counter (PPAC) was
placed in the front of the entrance to this
scattering chamber at Texas AM.
TAM: a scattering chamber filled with methane (CH4)
gas that was placed at the MARS focal plane.
